preamble: L-vocalization is a well-documented process where a lateral liquid transforms into a palatal glide. This shift is particularly prominent in the history of Romance languages and many English dialects.

examples.note: Latin /fl/ clusters consistently developed into /fj/ in Italian.
The historical palatal lateral /λ/ (derived from Latin /lj/ or /ll/) vocalized to a palatal glide /j/ in Modern French.
Various stages of liquid vocalization can be seen in the development of Portuguese color terms and common words.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
